votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by computing the set of lowercase characters and checking if its size matches the output.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by computing the set of lowercased characters and checking if its size matches the output.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by using a set to collect the lowercased characters of the string and checking if its length matches the output, which is equivalent to the Coq spec's existence of a duplicate-free list with the same properties.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute the number of distinct lowercase characters in a string. The Coq spec defines D as a set (list with NoDup) containing exactly the lowercase versions of all characters in s, and requires output to equal |D|. The Python code builds a set D by adding lower(c) for each character c in s, then checks if output equals len(D). The is_upper and lower functions match between Coq and Python (checking ASCII 65-90 for uppercase, adding 32 to convert). For any concrete string input, both will count the same number of distinct case-insensitive characters.

The Python code correctly computes the number of distinct lowercase characters in the string. It builds a set D of lowercase versions of all characters in s, then checks if output equals the size of D. This matches the Coq spec which requires D to be a set (NoDup) containing exactly the lowercase versions of characters in s, with output equal to |D|. The lower function implementations are equivalent (both convert uppercase A-Z to lowercase by adding 32 to ASCII value). The Python set automatically handles the NoDup requirement and the membership conditions from the Coq spec.

Both implementations count distinct lowercase versions of characters in the string. The Coq spec defines D as a set (list with NoDup) containing exactly the lowercase versions of all characters in s, and requires output to equal |D|. The Python code builds a set D by adding lower(c) for each character c in s, then checks if output equals len(D). Both use the same is_upper and lower functions (checking ASCII 65-90 and adding 32 for uppercase). The Python set automatically handles uniqueness like Coq's NoDup requirement. For any concrete string input, both will compute the same count of distinct lowercase characters.
Both compute the count of distinct characters after converting ASCII uppercase letters to lowercase; Python’s set-based implementation matches the Coq spec’s unique list set semantics.

Both compute the number of distinct characters after mapping ASCII A-Z to lowercase; Python builds the set of lowercased chars and compares its size to output, matching the Coq spec’s existence of a no-dup set D of lowercased characters with size equal to output.
Both define lower as mapping ASCII A–Z to a+32 and count the size of the set of lower(c) for c in s; output equals the number of distinct lowercased chars.
